title: GDPR Data Processing Agreement Obligation Control Matrix
exclusions: DPA obligations mapped to controls implemented under the master service agreement rather than the DPA-specific annex; regional DPA supplement obligations tracked separately.
evidence: DPA obligation control matrix showing each contractual obligation mapped to implementing controls with evidence links, compliance status, and gap remediation tracking.

compliance.assurance_rationale: GDPR Art.28(3) — Reviewed: SPL provides contributing evidence for this regulatory requirement. The search query directly addresses the monitoring objective specified by the clause.
compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that GDPR Art.28(3) is enforced — Splunk UC-22.1.30: GDPR Data Processing Agreement Obligation Control Matrix.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-22.1.30' running on gdpr_dpa_obligation_matrix.csv (processor_name, clause_id, included, evidence_url), procurement system exports, archived to the restricted audit_evidence index (default 7-year retention). Auto-drafted — SME review required.

controlTest.positiveScenario: Create a test DPA obligation entry with a control status of not_implemented and an overdue implementation date; verify the obligation matrix identifies the gap and alerts the contract manager.
controlTest.negativeScenario: Create a test DPA with all obligations mapped to implemented controls with evidence links; verify the matrix shows full compliance with no gaps reported.

dataSources: `gdpr_dpa_obligation_matrix.csv` (processor_name, clause_id, included, evidence_url), procurement system exports
spl:
| inputlookup gdpr_dpa_obligation_matrix.csv
| stats dc(eval(if(included="false", clause_id, null()))) as missing_clauses,
        values(eval(if(included="false", clause_id, null()))) as missing_list
  by processor_name
| where missing_clauses > 0
| join processor_name [
    | inputlookup gdpr_processor_register.csv
    | fields processor_name, go_live_date, dpa_signed
]
| eval live_without_dpa=if(dpa_signed!="true" AND isnotnull(go_live_date), 1, 0)
| table processor_name, missing_clauses, missing_list, dpa_signed, go_live_date, live_without_dpa
description: Article 28(3) spells out mandatory terms that must bind processors. This use case tracks which DPA clauses (sub-processing, assistance with DSARs, deletion/return, audit rights) are marked as included per processor and flags incomplete DPAs before go-live of new integrations.
value: Gives the responsible team auditor-ready evidence for GDPR compliance by automating regulatory compliance evidence. Replaces manual evidence gathering with a continuous Splunk search that produces structured, time-stamped artefacts suitable for regulatory examination and internal audit review.
implementation: (1) Encode Art. 28(3) obligations as rows in `gdpr_dpa_obligation_matrix.csv`; (2) integrate legal review workflow to set `included` and evidence URLs; (3) alert when any processor has missing mandatory clauses; (4) block go-live in ServiceNow when `live_without_dpa=1`; (5) refresh after each DPA amendment.
visualization: Table (incomplete DPAs), Bar chart (missing clauses by processor), Single value (processors with gaps). Key fields: `dpa_signed`, `go_live_date`, `live_without_dpa`, `missing_clauses`, `missing_list`, `processor_name`.
knownFalsePositives:
1. **DPA refresh cycle alignment** — Data Processing Agreements under review for standard annual refresh may temporarily show obligations mapped to the previous version while the new version is executed.

2. **Obligation inheritance from master agreements** — DPA obligations incorporated by reference from master service agreements may not appear in the DPA-specific control matrix but are contractually binding.

3. **Regional DPA variations** — Multinational processors may have regional DPA supplements that carry obligations not present in the master DPA; check regional addenda.

4. **Obligation mapping interpretation differences** — Different teams may map the same DPA clause to different control matrix entries based on interpretation; verify with legal for the canonical mapping.

5. **Transitional processing during DPA negotiation** — Processing that continues under an interim arrangement while a new DPA is negotiated may show against the expired DPA's obligation set.

**Operational suppression:** Maintain a lookup table (`gdpr_approved_exceptions.csv`) mapping known legitimate activities by process owner, approval reference, and expiry date. Filter the SPL results against this lookup to suppress documented exceptions and reduce alert fatigue. Review and rotate entries quarterly.
detailedImplementation:
## Prerequisites

• **Splunk**: Enterprise ≥9.2 or Splunk Cloud (current). Scheduling requires a search head not under memory pressure (check `index=_internal sourcetype=splunkd component=ProcessRunner` for OOM warnings before deploying a new scheduled search).
• **Lookups**: `gdpr_dpa_obligation_matrix.csv`, `gdpr_processor_register.csv` must be deployed to the search-tier app and refreshed on a documented schedule. For KV Store lookups, verify with `| rest /servicesNS/-/-/data/transforms/lookups | search name IN ('gdpr_dpa_obligation_matrix.csv','gdpr_processor_register.csv')`.
• **Search-head capacity**: Subsearch-based detections need `subsearch_maxtime` ≥ 60s and `subsearch_maxout` ≥ 50000 in `limits.conf`. For high-volume environments, tune `[search] max_searches_per_cpu` up from default.
• **Regulatory mapping**: This detection evidences clause `Art.28(3)`. The control objective is: "Evidence that GDPR Art.28(3) is enforced — Splunk UC-22.1.30: GDPR Data Processing Agreement Obligation Control Matrix." — every alert payload should include this text so downstream consumers know the compliance purpose.

## Step 1 — Configure data collection

Data collection is bespoke for this UC — no single canonical TA parses every source the detection relies on. Route data into the relevant indexes under the relevant sourcetypes, and document the ingestion path in the UC runbook so future engineers know where each field originates.


6 operational considerations apply to the rollout of this detection:


• **Hint 1:** Encode Art. 28. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 2:** obligations as rows in `gdpr_dpa_obligation_matrix.csv`. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 3:** integrate legal review workflow to set `included` and evidence URLs. Routing targets (SOC, compliance team, specific queue) must be documented with named on-call owners; an 'alerts go to the team' runbook fails at 3am on Sunday.

• **Hint 4:** alert when any processor has missing mandatory clauses. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 5:** block go-live in ServiceNow when `live_without_dpa=1`. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 6:** refresh after each DPA amendment. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

## Step 2 — Implement the search

For "GDPR Data Processing Agreement Obligation Control Matrix," the aggregation produces the result shape required by the GDPR control — each row is a summary record the regulation expects to be presentable at audit time.

This search correlates the outer dataset (index=`the outer`, sourcetype=`the primary`) with a subsearch on `processor_name` across the full search range. `type=inner` means the search only returns rows where `processor_name` exists in both datasets. This is the correct choice when the detection requires presence in both sources — a left-join would include orphaned rows that cannot be evaluated against the compliance rule.

Join caveat: Splunk's `| join` subsearch is capped at `max_searches_per_cpu * subsearch_maxout` results (default: 50000). If the inner subsearch exceeds this, rows are silently dropped and the resulting report will under-count. Check `index=_internal sourcetype=splunkd log_level=WARN subsearch` for truncation warnings. For inner searches expected to return >10000 rows, rewrite as `| stats` with a larger base search and post-filter, or use `| tstats` if a CIM datamodel covers the data.

**Schedule configuration** (`savedsearches.conf`):
```ini
[UC-22.1.30: GDPR Data Processing Agreement Obligation Control Matrix]
cron_schedule = 0 */6 * * *
dispatch.earliest_time = -24h@h
dispatch.latest_time = now
actions = summary_index
action.summary_index._name = summary_compliance
action.summary_index.marker = uc=22.1.30,reg=GDPR
```

## Step 3 — Validate deployment

**(a) Field extraction check:** Run the following to confirm the TA is extracting every field the detection relies on:

```spl
index=the_index sourcetype=the_sourcetype earliest=-1h
| fieldsummary
| where count=0
| table field
```

Any row returned is a field the TA claims to extract but isn't appearing in recent data — it's either a rename upstream, a TA version mismatch, or a data-format change.

**(b) Required-field presence:** This detection specifically needs: `dpa_signed`, `go_live_date`, `live_without_dpa`, `missing_clauses`, `missing_list`, `processor_name`. Run:

```spl
index=the_index sourcetype=the_sourcetype earliest=-1h
| head 100
| table dpa_signed, go_live_date, live_without_dpa, missing_clauses, missing_list, processor_name
```

Every row should have non-null values for the fields the detection's `where` clause filters on. Nulls cause silent drops in the result set.

**(d) Threshold calibration:** Before enabling as a production alert, run the base search with the threshold removed and profile the distribution of `missing_clauses`:

```spl
<base search> earliest=-30d
| stats perc50(missing_clauses) as p50 perc90(missing_clauses) as p90 perc99(missing_clauses) as p99 max(missing_clauses) as hi
```

The current threshold `0` should sit between p90 and p99 for a balanced signal-to-noise ratio. If it's below p50, expect heavy alert volume; if above p99, expect under-detection.

**(e) Negative test:** Generate a synthetic 'should not alert' event using the Splunk Sample Generator or a crafted `| makeresults` event and confirm the detection does NOT fire on it. A detection that fires on everything is equivalent to a detection that fires on nothing.

## Step 4 — Operationalize (dashboard and reporting)

Create a companion dashboard titled `UC-22.1.30: GDPR evidence` with the following panel layout:

**Panel 1 — Drill-through table (`incomplete DPAs`):** Full-width bottom row. Enable row-level drilldown to a secondary dashboard that expands each violation with its raw events, timeline, and related context. This is the panel the auditor will actually read — optimise it for scannability (avoid huge wrap-around fields).

**Panel 2 — Distribution (`missing clauses by processor`):** Shows the volumetric distribution across the primary dimension. Use `sort` so the worst offender is first — auditors focus on the tallest bar.

**Panel 3 — Single-value KPI (`processors with gaps`):** Place at top-left. Configure `underLabel` with the regulation clause (e.g. 'GDPR current violation count') and set a red colour range for any non-zero value. A zero reading is the steady state for a healthy control.


Time picker defaults: set to last 30 days with named presets for 24h, 7d, 30d, 90d, and 1y — the auditor needs all five windows without retyping. Enable scheduled PDF export for the full dashboard on the regulation's reporting cadence.

## Step 5 — Troubleshooting and regulation-specific caveats

**No results when violations exist:** The most likely cause is subsearch truncation (see Step 2). Inspect `index=_internal sourcetype=splunkd component=searchprocessor log_level=WARN subsearch` for messages like 'Output result count exceeded 50000.'

**Search times out:** Check the search job inspector (`Activity > Jobs > <this UC's last run> > Inspect`). The phase consuming the time (fetch, command, summary) indicates whether the issue is data-layer (too wide a window, unaccelerated datamodel), command-layer (expensive `transaction` or `eventstats`), or post-aggregation.


**GDPR-specific failure modes:**

• Lawful basis drift: a detection that worked when the processing activity had consent as its lawful basis becomes misleading when the business team silently changes the RoPA (Record of Processing Activities) entry to 'legitimate interest.' Every GDPR compliance search should reference the RoPA entry ID in its comments and re-review the detection when the RoPA is revised — at minimum annually.

• Data Subject Rights timers: Article 12(3) requires response to access/erasure/portability requests within one month. Detections that measure DSR fulfilment must use business-day clocks (not wall-clock 30 days) for regulators that enforce the distinction; the threshold value in the search should align with the local Supervisory Authority's interpretation, not the ISO 8601 literal.

• Cross-border transfer fragility: Schrems II invalidated the EU-US Privacy Shield in 2020 and the EU-US Data Privacy Framework (2023) replaced it; any detection involving transfer monitoring must key off the current adequacy decision list, not a hardcoded country allowlist. Maintain `gdpr_adequacy_list.csv` and refresh when the EDPB updates the adequacy register.

• Breach notification 72-hour window (Art. 33): the clock starts at awareness, not at the forensically-confirmed moment of compromise. Detections must capture the first-awareness event (SOC alert acknowledgment, first ticket open, first email notification) — not just the incident-resolved timestamp — or the notification SLA metric will understate breaches that took 72h+1h to confirm.
grandmaExplanation: We stay on top of data processing agreement obligation control matrix — so we can show we look after private information the way the privacy rules that apply to us require.
